Press enter or click to view image in full size

4 min read
Just now

--

A small experiment that changed how I think about “AI-friendly” code
Last week I gave an AI coding assistant the exact same task twice.
Build a simple form: a few inputs, some validation, a submit handler. Nothing fancy.
Once inside a fairly standard React + component library setup. Once in plain JavaScript with no framework at all.
Same prompt. Same requirements. Two very different experiences.
The plain JS version came out clean on the first try. The React version took three rounds of back-and-forth — a missing import here, a prop mismatch there, and one invented hook that didn’t exist in the library I was using. Nothing broken beyond repair, but noticeably more friction.
That gap is what this post is actually about. By the end, you’ll have a clearer sense of why that friction shows up, and — more usefully — what to actually do about it in your own day-to-day work with AI tools.
The pattern isn't about React being bad
I want to say this early, because it's easy to misread: this isn't a "framework X is dying" post. React, Angular, Vue — they all exist because they solve real problems for teams of humans working together over years. Consistent patterns, shared conventions, tooling that scales. That value doesn't disappear just because a model struggles with it.
What I noticed is narrower and more useful: the things that make a framework good for a team are often the exact things that make it harder for an AI to reason about in a single request.
Two reasons kept showing up when I dug into why.
Reason one: more files means more chances to guess wrong
In the React setup, the same form touched a component file, a validation schema file, a types file, and a couple of shared hooks. Every one of those is a place where the model has to guess a convention instead of seeing it.
A framework spreads logic across files for humans to organize. An AI has to hold all of that structure in its head at once, with no memory of yesterday’s decisions.
In the plain JS version, the entire form — markup, logic, validation — lived in one function. There was nothing to guess about because there was nowhere else the logic could be hiding.

That’s the actual mechanism, I think. It’s not that frameworks are complicated. It’s that they trade explicitness in one place for convention spread across many places — and conventions are exactly what a model can misremember or blend from a different codebase it’s seen.
Reason two: fewer decisions, fewer wrong turns
The plain JS version had basically one way to manage the form’s state — a plain object, updated directly. The React version had at least four reasonable options: local state, a form library, context, or a reducer. The model picked one, and it happened to be the one my specific component library handled slightly differently than the model expected.
This is worth sitting with: more flexibility in a tool is a genuine strength for a human developer and a genuine risk for an AI generating code in one shot. Humans use flexibility to make judgement calls informed by context. Models use it to roll the dice.
So what do you actually do with this?
Not “abandon your framework". A few small habits made a real difference for me:

• Ask for one file first, then split it. Let the AI write the whole feature as a single, flat block of logic. Refactor into your project’s file structure yourself afterward. You get correctness first, organisation second — instead of asking for both at once.

• State your state-management choice up front. Don’t let the model guess between four valid patterns. One sentence like “use local component state, no external form library” removes an entire category of possible mistakes.

• Point at real examples in your codebase, not just the framework name. “Use React” leaves too much open. “Match the pattern" gives the model something concrete to copy instead of infer.

• Treat the first draft as a rough draft, always. The plain JS version being clean on the first try was the exception, not something to expect by default. Budget for one review pass regardless of stack.
The actual takeaway
The interesting part isn’t that one stack "wins". It’s that the qualities we value in tools for humans — flexibility, convention, and structure spread across files — aren’t automatically the same qualities that help an AI generate correct code in a single pass. Knowing where that gap shows up lets you close it with a clearer prompt, instead of just more back-and-forth.
Next time you’re pairing with an AI tool on a framework-heavy feature, try stripping the ask down to one file first. It’s a small change, and it’s made my own sessions noticeably shorter.
